sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Melioidosis is a potentially fatal infection caused by *Burkholderia pseudomallei*, a bacterium found in soil and water in tropical and subtropical regions.

**Endemic in northern and tropical Western Australia** — particularly the Pilbara, Kimberley, and Gascoyne regions. Any mine site in these areas carries melioidosis risk.

**MANDATORY NOTIFIABLE DISEASE in Western Australia**

**Transmission**
- Inhalation of contaminated aerosols (soil dust, water droplets during wet season)
- Direct skin inoculation through wounds or abrasions in contact with soil or mud
- Ingestion of contaminated water (rare)
- **NOT transmitted person to person**

**Seasonal Pattern**
- Wet season (October–April) dramatically increases risk
- Flooding, heavy rainfall, and earthworks disturb contaminated soil
- Incubation: 1 day to several weeks (typically 2–21 days)

**Who Is at Risk**
- Workers with **diabetes** (highest risk — 50% of melioidosis cases)
- Renal disease, thalassaemia, hazardous alcohol use
- Immunosuppression (steroids, chemotherapy, HIV)
- Workers with skin wounds exposed to soil
- **Healthy workers can also be infected** — no exposure is safe in endemic areas

**Why It Is Frequently Missed**
- Mimics sepsis, pneumonia, or other common infections
- Standard antibiotics used empirically (amoxicillin-clavulanate, ciprofloxacin) are **ineffective** — must use meropenem or ceftazidime

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Four Main Presentations**
**1. Acute Septicaemia (most dangerous)**
- High fever, rigors, night sweats
- Rapid deterioration — can progress to septic shock within hours
- Absent localising symptoms initially
- Mortality >40% without appropriate treatment

**2. Pneumonia (most common)**
- Fever, cough, haemoptysis
- Dyspnoea, chest pain
- May resemble TB — upper lobe involvement common
- SpO2 may be severely reduced

**3. Localised Infection**
- Skin ulcers, pustules, nodules
- Lymph node swelling and suppuration
- Joint or bone infection (mimics septic arthritis/osteomyelitis)
- Parotid abscess — especially in children

**4. Chronic Melioidosis (weeks to months)**
- Weight loss, night sweats, productive cough
- Closely mimics tuberculosis
- May follow apparently recovered acute illness

**Red Flags in Endemic Area**
- Sepsis with no obvious source in a diabetic or immunocompromised worker
- Pneumonia not responding to standard antibiotics within 48 hours
- Any febrile illness in Pilbara/Kimberley worker during or after wet season

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
**Treatment Must Be Specific — Standard Antibiotics Fail**
*B. pseudomallei* is intrinsically resistant to:
- Penicillin G
- Ampicillin
- First- and second-generation cephalosporins
- Aminoglycosides
- Macrolides
- **Amoxicillin-clavulanate** (commonly used for community infections — NOT effective)

**Effective antibiotics:**
- IV meropenem (first-line intensive phase)
- IV ceftazidime (alternative if meropenem unavailable)
- Eradication phase (oral, hospital-directed): trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (co-trimoxazole)

**Field Treatment Limitation**
- IV meropenem is not available at most mine sites
- The role of the mine site clinician is: **recognise, resuscitate, and evacuate urgently**
- Contact RFDS for advice — they may be able to bring meropenem on the evacuation aircraft

**Notification**
- Notify WA Health on **clinical suspicion** — do not wait for laboratory confirmation
- WA Health Notifiable Disease: (08) 9222 4177

**Blood Cultures Critical**
- Take blood cultures before antibiotics if possible — do NOT delay antibiotics for this
- Document clearly on request: "Suspected melioidosis — Pilbara/Kimberley exposure"
- Standard laboratory processing may not identify *B. pseudomallei* without specific flagging

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
**Immediate Actions**
1. **Treat as sepsis** — see sepsis-septic-shock guideline for ABCDE approach
2. **IV access** — two large-bore cannulae
3. **Blood cultures** — before antibiotics if can be done without delay
4. **Call RFDS immediately** — IV meropenem required; must be evacuated urgently
5. **Notify WA Health** — mandatory notifiable on suspicion

**Sepsis Resuscitation**
- IV fluid bolus: Hartmann's 500 mL over 15 minutes; reassess and repeat if hypotension persists
- Oxygen: titrate to SpO2 >94%
- Cardiac monitoring
- Blood glucose — hypoglycaemia common in sepsis with diabetes

**Empirical Antibiotics (While Awaiting Evacuation)**
If evacuation is significantly delayed and septic shock present:
- Ceftriaxone 2 g IV (broad-spectrum coverage pending meropenem)
- NOTE: ceftriaxone is NOT definitive for melioidosis but provides partial cover for concurrent organisms
- **RFDS will bring meropenem if aware of suspected melioidosis** — communicate clearly

**Fever Management**
- Paracetamol 1 g IV/PO for fever >38.5°C
- Active cooling if temperature >40°C (see hyperthermia guideline)

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
**Septic Shock**
If hypotension not responding to 2 L fluid:
- Noradrenaline (via large peripheral or central IV) — contact RFDS for direction
- Target MAP >65 mmHg
- Vasopressin as second vasopressor if available

**Pneumonia with Respiratory Failure**
- High-flow oxygen
- NIPPV if conscious and cooperative
- Intubation if GCS <8 or respiratory failure progressing
- Tidal volumes 6 mL/kg (lung protective)

**Localised Abscess**
- Do NOT incise and drain without surgical guidance
- Large abscesses require surgical management at hospital
- Cover with moist dressing

**Glucose Management**
- Diabetic patients with melioidosis commonly have severe hyperglycaemia AND hypoglycaemia
- Regular BGL monitoring every 1–2 hours
- Treat hypoglycaemia aggressively

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Treatment Duration**
Hospital management involves:
- Intensive phase: IV meropenem for 10–14 days minimum
- Eradication phase: oral TMP-SMX (co-trimoxazole) for 3–6 months
- Premature cessation leads to relapse

**Prevention**
- Avoid contact between open wounds/skin abrasions and soil or muddy water in endemic areas
- Wear waterproof boots and gloves during wet-season earthworks
- Diabetic workers should be particularly vigilant
- No vaccine currently available

**WA-Specific Context**
- Pilbara and Kimberley regions have among the highest melioidosis rates globally per capita
- Cases peak January–March (peak wet season)
- Any febrile illness presenting in or shortly after returning from these regions should have melioidosis considered
